skill.name: address-github-comments
skill.description: Guides an AI agent through reviewing, planning, fixing, and responding to GitHub pull request comments with the gh CLI.
skill.summary: Address GitHub pull request feedback using a structured gh CLI workflow.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged shell-style command examples and a weak-cryptography pattern. The cryptography finding at SKILL.md:3 is a false positive because the file contains only metadata text, while the gh CLI commands are legitimate for the skill purpose but still require authenticated external command execution and can post to GitHub. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated GitHub CLI Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to use gh CLI commands, including viewing pull request comments and posting a pull request comment. This is appropriate for the stated workflow, but it executes external commands with the user authenticated to GitHub and can modify repository discussions. Users should review generated commands and comment bodies before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are directly present and match the skill purpose. The risk is moderate because authenticated gh commands can change GitHub state, but there is no evidence of malicious arguments or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Is False Positive
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported a weak cryptographic algorithm at the description line. The referenced line is front matter describing use of the gh CLI and contains no cryptographic function, algorithm selection, hashing, encryption, or secret handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is plain metadata text. I found no cryptographic code or crypto-related instruction in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Unspecified Custom Script Option
low_findings.description: The workflow says to use a custom script if available to list threads. This is not inherently malicious, but it could encourage running unreviewed local automation if an agent follows the instruction too broadly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is present but does not name a script, source, or execution command. The concern depends on local repository context and should be handled by user confirmation.

low_findings.title: Inline Command Mentions Are Safe Documentation
low_findings.description: Several static findings came from inline markdown references to gh or gh auth login. These are documentation examples for normal GitHub CLI setup, not hidden shell execution or dynamic command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is markdown documentation and prerequisite guidance. It does not contain variable interpolation, downloads, privilege escalation, or shell metacharacter use.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains shell command examples for gh auth status, gh pr view --comments, and gh pr comment. These commands should be treated as user-approved actions because they rely on local authentication and can read or write GitHub pull request data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell examples are explicit and require the external gh CLI. They appear legitimate for the workflow, but they are still privileged external commands.

content.value_statement: Pull request feedback can be hard to track across review threads and issue comments. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a structured gh CLI workflow to inspect, plan, fix, verify, and respond.
actual_capabilities: Checks whether the GitHub CLI is authenticated before starting work.
Fetches pull request comments for the current branch using gh pr view.
Organizes comments and review threads into a fix plan.
Prompts for user confirmation before addressing many comments.
Guides code changes for selected review feedback.
Posts a follow-up pull request comment after fixes are complete.
limitations: Requires the gh CLI to be installed and authenticated.
Does not resolve GitHub review threads through the API by itself.
Does not decide large batches of fixes without user confirmation.
Depends on repository context and available pull request metadata.

use_cases.title: Review Fix Pass
use_cases.description: Turn pull request comments into a clear fix list, then apply selected changes in a controlled order.

use_cases.title: Maintainer Response Workflow
use_cases.description: Review open feedback, prepare responses, and post a concise update after changes are complete.

use_cases.title: Team Review Triage
use_cases.description: Group comments by priority and ask for confirmation before handling a large review queue.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect PR Comments
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for finding current feedback.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the address-github-comments skill to inspect the current branch pull request comments. Summarize each comment and do not make changes yet.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Review Fixes
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a safe plan before editing files.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the address-github-comments skill to categorize the PR feedback, identify likely files to change, and ask me which items to address first.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Selected Comments
prompt_templates.scenario: Apply confirmed changes from review feedback.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the address-github-comments skill to address the confirmed review comments. Read surrounding code first, apply focused fixes, and verify the result.

prompt_templates.title: Complete Review Cycle
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced end-to-end workflow with verification and response.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the address-github-comments skill to inspect all PR feedback, propose a grouped plan, implement approved fixes, run relevant checks, and draft a final GitHub comment.

output_examples.input: Inspect the open PR comments and tell me what needs work.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns a grouped list of review comments, affected areas, likely fixes, and questions that need user confirmation.
The assistant updates the relevant documentation, verifies the change, and prepares a short response for the pull request.

output_examples.input: Post an update after the selected fixes are complete.
output_examples.output: The assistant drafts a concise status comment that says which feedback was addressed and what checks were run.

best_practices: Confirm the target pull request before running gh commands that read or post comments.
Review each comment in surrounding code context before changing files.
Run relevant tests or checks before posting that feedback is addressed.
anti_patterns: Do not post generic resolution comments before verifying the fix.
Do not run custom scripts from a repository without reviewing what they do.
Do not batch unrelated review fixes into one unclear change.

faq.question: Does this skill require GitHub authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It expects the gh CLI to be authenticated before reading or posting pull request comments.

faq.question: Can it work with issue comments as well as PR comments?
faq.answer: The description includes review and issue feedback, but the documented commands focus on pull request comments.

faq.question: Will it automatically choose every fix?
faq.answer: No. The workflow tells the agent to ask for confirmation when there are many comments.

faq.question: Can it resolve GitHub review threads directly?
faq.answer: Not by itself. The documented workflow posts a pull request comment after fixes are complete.

faq.question: Is it safe to run in private repositories?
faq.answer: Use normal repository security controls. Review commands and comment text before posting to GitHub.

faq.question: What tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
